steps.annotation: This program starts by loading the input/output library
The computer begins executing instructions inside main
The computer runs this instruction, printing Hello to the screen
A program is a list of text instructions the computer follows in order
Each line is one instruction; the computer reads them top to bottom
Step 2 runs after Step 1 because instructions execute sequentially
C++ source code is saved in a file ending with .cpp
The .cpp file contains all the program instructions as plain text
You write code in the .cpp file, then compile and run it
The compiler (g++) converts your .cpp source file into an executable
g++ reads this human readable code and translates it to machine code
After compilation, hello.exe contains the machine version of this line
Running the .exe starts execution at main, line by line
The CPU executes this instruction first, printing Hello
Then executes this instruction, ending the program with exit code 0
This program prints two lines of output to the terminal
cout sends "Hello" to the terminal window where you see it
Output appears in the terminal, the text window below your code
The program prints a prompt asking the user to type something
Program pauses; user types a number in the terminal and presses Enter
The typed value is now stored in age and printed back
This line loads the iostream library so cout and cin are available
Without the include, cout on this line would cause a compiler error
cout works here because iostream was included on line 1
This line lets you write cout instead of the longer std::cout
Without "using namespace std", you would need std::cout here
Every C++ program must have main; execution always starts here
Code inside main runs first when you launch the program
Every statement must end with a semicolon or the compiler gives an error
This statement also needs its semicolon to mark the end
The opening brace { starts a code block, grouping statements together
Everything between { and } belongs to this block
The block opened with { on this line
The closing brace } ends the block; every { must have a matching }
The program does its work, then needs to signal it finished
return 0 tells the operating system the program ended successfully
Everything after // on a line is a comment, ignored by the compiler
Comments explain code to humans; the computer only runs x = x + 1
Extra spaces between tokens are fine; the compiler ignores whitespace
No spaces is also valid; whitespace is optional around tokens
Indentation makes code readable but does not change what it does
Different indentation still compiles; it is style, not syntax
This program demonstrates printing text to the terminal
cout is the output stream; it sends "hello" to the terminal screen
x holds the value 5 in memory
The << operator sends the value of x (5) to cout for display
This program prints a text string to the screen
Text in double quotes is a string literal; cout prints it exactly as written
cout can print numbers directly; 5 appears on screen as the digit 5
Prints 42 right after 5 with no space, producing "542" on screen
endl moves the cursor to a new line after printing "line one"
"line two" appears on a separate line because endl came before it
The escape sequence \n inside a string also creates a new line
\n works like endl but is typed inside the string quotes
Two variables ready to print on one line
Chain multiple << to print several values in one statement: outputs "1 2"
id holds the integer value 42
printf takes a format string and fills %d with the value of id (42)
n is an int variable holding 7
%d is the format specifier for int; it gets replaced by n, printing "count = 7"
name is a C++ string holding "ada"
%s formats a C string; .c_str() converts the C++ string for printf
The \n at the end of the format string moves to a new line after printing
"world" appears on the next line because the previous \n started a new line
The first argument to printf is the format string that controls the output layout
"score = %d\n" is the template; %d marks where the number goes
Three %d placeholders are filled left to right by the extra arguments 2, 3, 5
Result: "2 + 3 = 5"; each %d matches the next argument in order
A variable is a named memory box; n is a box labeled "n" holding 5
You can change the value inside the box; n now holds 6
Reading n retrieves the current value (6) from its memory box
Every variable has a name; "counter" describes what this box tracks
"total" is a different name for a different box; names should be meaningful
Every variable has a type that says what kind of data it can hold
double holds decimal numbers, int holds whole numbers, string holds text
The type must match the data: text goes in string, not int
Declaring a variable creates an empty box; type first, then the name
Pattern: type name; creates the box without storing a value yet
Initialization: type name = value; creates the box AND stores a value at once
"ada" is immediately placed in the name box when it is created
x holds the value 5 in its memory box
Using x in an expression reads the value (5) from the box without changing it
x starts with value 5
Assignment overwrites the old value; x now holds 10 (5 is gone)
Single = is assignment: it stores 5 into x
Double == is comparison: it checks if x equals 5 (returns true/false)
A variable must be declared before any line that uses it
This line can use x because x was declared on the line above
int stores whole numbers (no decimals): 7, 0, 42, or negative like below
int can hold negative whole numbers too, but never 3.14 or "hello"
double stores decimal (floating point) numbers like 3.14
Use double whenever you need fractional precision, not int
string holds a sequence of characters, meaning text data
Any text in double quotes can be stored in a string variable
bool can only hold two values: true or false (yes or no)
Booleans are used for conditions and flags; prints as 1 (true) or 0 (false)
char holds exactly one character, using single quotes around it
'7' is the character seven, not the number 7; char holds one letter or symbol
5 is an int literal: a whole number written directly in the code
Int literals have no quotes and no decimal point; just digits
3.14 is a double literal: a number with a decimal point in the code
The decimal point tells the compiler this is a double, not an int
"hello" is a string literal: text enclosed in double quotes
String literals can appear directly in cout without storing in a variable
'A' is a char literal: a single character in single quotes
Single quotes for char ('A'), double quotes for string ("A"): different types
true is a bool literal; it is a keyword, not a string (no quotes)
false is the other bool literal; true and false are the only two bool values
The string type requires #include <string> at the top of the file
Without the include, using string here would cause a compiler error
Declare a box to receive the user input
cin reads from the terminal; the program pauses until the user types a value
After the user types and presses Enter, n holds their input
x is an empty box waiting for input
The >> operator extracts the next value from input and stores it in x
A prompt tells the user what to type
Execution stops here and waits until the user types something and presses Enter
Two boxes declared to receive two separate input values
User types "3 7"; whitespace (space or enter) separates the two values
cin parses the input to match the variable type; digits become an int
For a string variable, cin reads one word (stops at whitespace)
Three boxes ready for three input values
Chain >> to read multiple values in one statement; each >> grabs the next token
A string box to hold a full line of text including spaces
getline reads the entire line including spaces, unlike cin >> which stops at a space
The + operator adds two values; 3 + 4 produces 7, stored in sum
Prints 7, the result of the addition
The minus operator subtracts: 10 minus 3 gives 7
Prints 7, the result of the subtraction
Two values to multiply together
The * operator multiplies: 5 * 3 produces 15
n holds 10, the dividend
The / operator divides: 10 / 2 produces 5
The % (modulo) operator gives the remainder: 7 divided by 3 leaves remainder 1
Prints 1, the remainder when dividing 7 by 3
When both sides are int, division truncates: 5/2 gives 2, not 2.5
Prints 2 because int division drops the decimal part entirely
* runs before + (like in math): 2*3=6 first, then 1+6=7
Prints 7, not 9, because multiplication has higher precedence than addition
Parentheses force 1+2 to happen first (=3), then 3*3=9
Prints 9 because parentheses overrode the default operator order
total starts at 0
+= is shorthand for total = total + 5; total becomes 5
-= is shorthand for total = total - 2; total becomes 3
i starts at 0
++ adds 1 to i; shorthand for i = i + 1, so i becomes 1
n starts at 10
Subtracts 1 from n; shorthand for n = n - 1, so n becomes 9
x holds 5, and we want to check if it equals something
== tests equality: returns true if both sides are the same value
x holds 3, which is not zero
!= tests "not equal": true when the two sides differ (3 is not 0)
< tests "less than": the loop runs while i is less than n
Prints 0 1 2; loop stops when i reaches 3 because 3 < 3 is false
Two values to compare
> tests "greater than": true because 10 is larger than 3
i equals count; we want to include that case
<= tests "less than or equal": true when i is smaller OR the same as count
score is 75, and the passing threshold is 50
>= tests "greater than or equal": true because 75 is at least 50
A comparison like x == 5 produces a bool result: true or false
The bool value can be stored in a variable and used later in conditions
Two values that are both positive
&& means AND: the whole condition is true only if BOTH sides are true
x is zero, y is not
|| means OR: true if EITHER side (or both) is true
found is false, meaning the item was not located
! flips the bool: !false becomes true, so the body runs
if checks the condition in parentheses; if true, the body runs
This line only executes when x > 0 is true (3 > 0 is true)
x is negative, so this condition is false; the if body is skipped
else runs when the if condition is false, providing an alternative path
This prints because x is negative and the else branch was taken
First check: is x positive? No (x is 0), so move to next check
else if chains another condition: is x negative? No, continue
Final else catches everything remaining; x must be zero
The comparison produces a bool (true/false) value stored in ok
if needs a bool condition; ok is true, so the body runs
Always use braces {} around the if body, even for a single line
Braces prevent bugs when you later add more lines to this block
while checks the condition before each iteration; repeats body while true
Body runs 5 times with i = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4; prints "0 1 2 3 4"
i increases each iteration; when i reaches 5, the condition becomes false
do runs the body at least once before checking the condition
User enters a number; the body always executes at least once
Condition checked after body; if n is still negative, the loop repeats
for combines init, condition, and step in one line: compact loop syntax
Runs 5 times printing 0 1 2 3 4; for loop is the most common loop form
The init part (int i = 0) runs exactly once, before the first iteration
i starts at 0 because init set it; init never runs again
The condition (i < 5) is tested before each iteration, including the first
Body only runs if condition is true; when i becomes 5, loop stops
The body runs first, then the step (i++) runs after
The step (i++) runs after each body execution, before the next condition check
This loop would run 100 times, but break can exit it early
break immediately exits the loop; only prints 0 1 2 3 4 5 6
continue skips the rest of this iteration and jumps to the next one
Prints 0 1 3 4; the value 2 is skipped because continue bypassed this line
Outer loop runs 3 times (once per row)
Inner loop runs 4 times per outer iteration; total body runs 3x4=12 times
while(true) creates an infinite loop; the condition is always true
Without break or a changing condition, the loop would run forever
A function is a named block of code you can call by name
This code runs whenever greet() is called
Calling greet() jumps to the function, runs its body, then returns here
Pattern: returnType name(params) { body } defines a function
The function takes two ints and returns their sum as an int
Calling add(3, 4) passes 3 and 4 as arguments; the result (7) is stored in sum
Prints 7, the value returned by the function call
Parameter x is a local copy of whatever value was passed in
Changing x only changes the local copy; the caller variable n stays 5
The return type (int) says this function sends back an integer
return sends the computed value (25) back to the caller
void means this function does not return a value; it just does work
The function prints output but sends nothing back to the caller
A prototype declares the function before main so the compiler knows it exists
square is called here; the compiler trusts the prototype
The full definition appears after main; matches the prototype
A prototype only needs the return type, name, and parameter types (names optional)
This tells the compiler greet takes a string and returns nothing
local is created when f() starts running
When f returns, local is destroyed; its memory box no longer exists
secret only exists inside f; it is local to that function
The caller (main) cannot see or use variables from inside f
Without &, x is a copy of the caller value; a separate memory box
This changes the copy, not the original; n in main stays 5
Prints 5 because the function only modified its own local copy
Pass by value: x gets a copy of n, they are separate boxes
Mutating the parameter does NOT change the caller variable
n is still 5; the function cannot affect main variables by value
& makes x an alias (another name) for the same memory box as n
Changing x changes n because they share the same memory box
Prints 6 because & let the function modify the caller variable directly
void f(int &x) means x shares the caller memory box, not a copy
When called, x and n point to the same box; changes via x affect n
Because & was used, mutating x here directly changes the caller n
n is now 6; the mutation persisted because & shares the same box
OK: n is a real variable with a memory box that & can alias

steps.code: // increment(5);  // ERROR: cannot bind literal to &
steps.annotation: A literal like 5 has no memory box, so & cannot create an alias to it
const & shares the box (no copy) but prevents any modifications
Reading is allowed; writing like user.username = "X" would cause a compiler error
A reference means x and n are two different names for the same box
n shows 6: one box, two names (x inside the function, n in main)
An array holds a sequence of N values stored in a row in memory
Access each value by its index position: 0, 1, 2, 3, 4
type arr[N] declares an array with N slots; here 5 int slots are created
Slots start uninitialized; you must assign values before reading them
Brace initialization fills all slots at once: arr[0]=1, arr[1]=2, arr[2]=3
Prints "1 2 3", confirming each slot was initialized
Array has 3 elements at positions 0, 1, and 2
arr[0] is always the first element; arrays start counting at 0, not 1
i is the index, pointing to position 2 in the array
arr[i] accesses element at index i; with i=2, this prints 30
Three slots: arr[0]=10, arr[1]=20, arr[2]=30; indexing starts at 0
First element is at index 0, not index 1; prints 10
Array has N=4 elements at indices 0, 1, 2, 3
The last element is always at index N minus 1; prints arr[3] which is 20
Array has 3 elements: indices 0, 1, 2 are valid
arr[3] is past the end; accessing it causes undefined behavior (crash or garbage)
Array size must be a constant known at compile time
The array is created with exactly 100 slots; this size cannot change later
You cannot return an array; instead pass it as a parameter to fill
The function modifies the array directly since arrays pass by reference
A for loop with i from 0 to N minus 1 visits every array element
arr[i] accesses each element in turn: arr[0], arr[1], ... arr[4]
All three slots start at 0
arr[i] = v writes value v into the slot at index i; arr[2] becomes 99
To pass an array, use type arr[] in the parameter list; always pass size n too
The array name (data) and its size (3) are passed to the function
Arrays are always passed by reference; the function works on the caller array
Changes to arr[0] inside the function directly modify the caller data array
A struct groups related fields together under one name
Each field is a named data slot; Student bundles name and age
struct Name { fields }; creates a new custom type called Point
Point has two fields: x and y, both integers
The semicolon after } is required to complete the struct definition
Each field declaration follows the pattern: type fieldName;
Every field has its own type and name, just like regular variables
The struct body is enclosed in braces { }
The closing }; with a semicolon is mandatory; forgetting ; is a common error
Using the struct type name to declare a variable creates an instance of it
p now has its own x and y fields, ready to store data
The dot operator . accesses a field inside a struct variable
p.x reads the x field of p; the dot connects the struct to its member
x.field = v writes a value into the named field of the struct
Each field is written independently using dot notation
Store the value 3 in the x field of p
cout << p.x reads the field value (3) and prints it to the terminal
A struct can contain an array field; numbers holds 5 doubles
Mix of array and scalar fields in the same struct is allowed
Chain dot and bracket: d.numbers accesses the array, then [0] the first element
data.arr[i] reads element i of the array field inside the struct
Name list[N] creates an array of N structs; each element is a full Point
Each slot in the array is an independent struct with its own fields
list[i].field chains array index and field access in one expression
Each array element has its own set of fields; list[1].x is separate from list[0].x
Pass by value: p is a complete copy of the caller struct
Changing the copy has no effect on the original; pt.x stays 3
Prints 3; the whole struct was copied, so the original is unchanged
& shares the struct; p is an alias for the caller variable pt
Changes go directly into the caller struct; pt.x becomes 99
& lets the function modify the caller struct directly
This change persists in the caller because & shares the same memory
Prints 99; the mutation via & persisted after the function returned
Passing an array of structs lets the function fill the caller array
Arrays pass by reference, so writing to list[i] modifies the caller array
Writing to list[i].field inside the function persists in the caller array
Prints "C++" because the array was passed by reference
const & shares the struct efficiently but prevents any modification
Reading fields is fine; trying to write p.x = 5 would cause a compiler error
Tracing means mentally stepping through code line by line, tracking values
At this line, you update your mental note: x was 3, now x is 5
You predict the output (5) by reading from your tracked state
Track: x = 5 (write it down or keep a mental note)
Track: x = 5, y = 10; keep a running table of every variable value
Trace: x = 5 (initial value)
On reassignment, cross out the old value: x = 5 -> 10; latest is 10
Track i each iteration: i=0 (body runs), i=1 (body runs), i=2 (body runs)
After body, step runs: i becomes 3, condition 3<3 is false, loop ends
Evaluate the condition: 7 > 5 is true, so take the if branch
This branch runs because the condition was true; else is skipped entirely
At a function call, mentally enter a new frame with its own local variables
New frame: x = 5 (the argument value); trace inside f now
local exists only inside this frame; track it while f is running
When f returns, the frame is destroyed; erase local from your trace table
With &, x and n share the same box; changes to x affect n directly
Update your trace: n was 5, now n = 6 (because x IS n via &)
Back in main, n = 6; the & mutation persisted after incr returned
Trace: arr = {1, 2, 3}
slot is & alias to arr[0]; slot = 99 changes arr to {99, 2, 3}
Trace: x = 5
y copies the current value of x; trace: x = 5, y = 5 (separate boxes)
Trace: arr = {0, 7, 0}; update slot 1 in your table
arr[1] is 7, so arr[2] = 7+1 = 8; trace: arr = {0, 7, 8}
Trace each iteration: i=1 sum=1, i=2 sum=3, i=3 sum=6, i=4 sum=10
Final state after loop: sum = 10 (1+2+3+4)
Evaluate: 7 > 5 is true, so take the if branch
Trace: label = 1 (the else branch is never entered)
Call square: enter new frame with x=5, compute 5*5=25, return 25
Back in main: r = 25 (the returned value); prints 25
Pass by value: f_val gets a copy; n stays 5 after the call
Pass by reference: f_ref shares n via &; n becomes 99 after the call
Start max at first element (3.2); compare against remaining elements
Trace: i=1 7.1>3.2 yes max=7.1, i=2 no, i=3 9.4>7.1 yes max=9.4, i=4 no
Final max_val = 9.4, the largest element in the array
& means p is an alias for a; changes inside shift affect a in main
Trace: a.x was 3, now 4; a.y was 4, now 5 (both mutated via &)
Each iteration compares numbers[i] to current mystery (running max)
Only updates mystery when a larger value is found; trace: 2.0->8.0 (final)
To write a struct, start with struct followed by the type name and {
List each field with its type and name, one per line
Close with }; to complete the custom type definition
Each field follows the pattern: type fieldName; just like a variable declaration
Choose the type that matches the data: string for text, int for whole numbers
The struct body opens with {
The closing }; with semicolon is mandatory; without ; the code will not compile
Pick int for IDs and counts, string for text, double for monetary amounts
The spec says "title" is text, so string is the right type choice
Prices need decimals, so double (not int) is the correct type
Fields can appear in any order; the compiler does not enforce a sequence
Whether id is first or last does not change how the struct works
void means this function reads data but returns nothing
The function does its work through side effects (filling the array)
Type list[] passes the struct array so the function can fill it
The function writes into the caller array through the passed reference
The int count parameter tells the function how many items to read
count controls the loop bound; only count elements are read, not the whole array
The for loop uses count as the upper bound to read exactly count items
Each iteration reads one struct element at index i
cin >> list[i].field reads user input directly into the struct field
Each field gets its own cin statement to read from the terminal
One cin per field: each struct field needs its own input statement
Three fields means three cin lines per loop iteration
An optional prompt before cin tells the user what to type
The cin follows the prompt, reading the value the user types
Every program needs int main() as the entry point
return 0 at the end signals the program completed successfully
const int MAX = 100 creates a constant; its value can never change
MAX is used as the array size; const ensures it stays fixed
Declare an array of your struct type with MAX capacity
Each slot is a full struct instance with all its fields
Declare int count to track how many items the user wants to enter
The user provides the actual number of items at runtime
cin >> count reads N from the user, setting how many items to process
count now holds the user provided number, used to control loops
Call the read function, passing your array and the count from the user
The function fills the array; main orchestrates by calling it
A print loop iterates from 0 to count to display each item
Inside the loop, print each struct element using printf or cout
printf uses %d for the int id and %s for the string title
Each format specifier matches a field value passed as an argument
name is a C++ string, but printf needs a C style string
.c_str() converts the C++ string to a C string that %s can format
This mock exam struct has three double fields: mystery, a, and b
who_am_i takes a struct by reference and computes mystery
mystery is set to the sum of a and b; this is a simple addition pattern
Initialize mystery to the first element before scanning the rest
Compare each element to current max; update mystery when larger found
After the loop, mystery holds the maximum value from the array
Initialize the accumulator to 0 before summing
Loop through all 5 elements, adding each to mystery
Each iteration adds the current element; mystery ends as the total sum
Check which value is larger to avoid a negative result
If a > b, subtract b from a
Otherwise subtract a from b; mystery is always the absolute difference
Initialize counter to 0 before counting
Check if each element is positive
Add 1 for each positive number; mystery ends as the count of positives
